walkthrough.title: Sort the Card Types
walkthrough.text: Let's get Race for the Galaxy set up! First, find and separate three types of cards. The game cards are the big deck with worlds and developments — these have different colored borders and symbols. The starting world cards have a special icon on the back. And each player needs a set of seven action cards with phase numbers on them. Set the action cards aside for now.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the VP Chip Pool
walkthrough.text: Now let's create the victory point chip pool. Count out twelve VP chips per player. So for a two-player game, use twenty-four chips. For three players, use thirty-six. For four players, use forty-eight. Mix the one-point and five-point chips together and place them in a shared pool in the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Game Deck
walkthrough.text: Take all the game cards — everything except the starting worlds and action cards — and give them a thorough shuffle. This deck contains both worlds and developments that players will draw, play, and use as currency throughout the game. Place the shuffled deck face down in a central spot.

walkthrough.title: Place the Phase Summary
walkthrough.text: Find the phase summary card and place it where everyone can see it. This card lists all five phases and their bonuses. It is an essential reference, especially for your first few games. Players will check this card frequently to remember what each phase does.

walkthrough.title: Designate a Discard Area
walkthrough.text: Cards will be discarded frequently — when paying costs, when excess cards are drawn, and when cards are consumed. Keep the discard pile face down and separate from the draw deck.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Worlds
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the five starting world cards and deal one face down to each player. Return any extras to the box without looking at them. Each starting world has different abilities that will shape your early strategy. Flip your starting world face up and place it in front of you — this is the first card in your tableau.

walkthrough.title: Give Each Player Action Cards
walkthrough.text: These are numbered with roman numerals from one through five, with two variants for phases one and four. You will select one of these cards each round to choose which phase you want to activate. Keep them in a neat pile in front of you.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal six cards from the game deck to each player. Pick up your cards and look at them. These are your starting options — both potential tableau cards and potential currency. You will need to discard two of these six cards before the game begins.

walkthrough.title: Discard Down to Four
walkthrough.text: Now comes your first decision. Look at your six cards and your starting world. Choose two cards to discard and keep four. Consider which cards work well with your starting world's abilities. Cards in hand serve double duty — they can be played into your tableau or discarded as payment for other cards. Choose wisely.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Player Areas
walkthrough.text: Each player needs space for three things. Your tableau is the area in front of you where played cards go — your starting world is already there. Your hand is the four cards you just kept — hold them privately. And your action cards sit in a separate pile where you can easily select from them each round. Make sure everyone has room.

walkthrough.title: Begin the First Round
walkthrough.text: You are ready to play! The game begins immediately. All players simultaneously select one action card from their set and place it face down. When everyone has chosen, flip them all face up. Only the phases that were selected by at least one player will be carried out this round, resolved in numerical order. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: The Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Race for the Galaxy is played in rounds. Each round has the same structure. First, every player secretly selects one action card and places it face down. Then all cards are revealed simultaneously. Only the phases that at least one player selected are carried out. Phases are resolved in order: Phase one Explore, Phase two Develop, Phase three Settle, Phase four Consume, Phase five Produce. After all active phases resolve, players take back their action cards and a new round begins.

walkthrough.title: The Chooser Bonus
walkthrough.text: Here is the twist that makes phase selection so interesting. The player who selected a phase gets a special bonus during that phase. But every player participates in every active phase, not just the chooser. So if you choose Develop, you get a discount on developing, but your opponents also get to develop — just without the bonus. This creates a constant tension: choosing a phase benefits you but also benefits everyone else.

walkthrough.title: Cards Are Everything
walkthrough.text: There is no money, no resources, no separate tokens for payment. When you want to play a development or settle a world, you pay by discarding cards from your hand. Every card in your hand is both a potential play and potential payment for something else. This dual-use tension is the heart of the game.

walkthrough.title: Phase One: Explore
walkthrough.text: The base action is to draw two cards and keep one, discarding the other. But there are two different Explore action cards you can choose. Explore plus five lets you draw seven cards but still keep only one. Explore plus one plus one lets you draw three cards and keep two. If you are not the one who selected Explore, you just get the base draw two keep one.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two: Develop
walkthrough.text: During Develop, you may play one development card from your hand. Developments have a diamond-shaped cost icon in the upper left. To play one, discard that many cards from your hand as payment. For example, a three-cost development requires you to discard three other cards. The chooser bonus is a one-card discount — so that same three-cost development would only cost two cards if you chose the Develop phase.

walkthrough.title: Phase Three: Settle
walkthrough.text: During Settle, you may play one world card from your hand. Worlds come in two types. Non-military worlds have a circle with a number — pay that many cards from your hand, just like developments. Military worlds have a red circle — you do not pay cards at all. Instead, you need enough military strength in your tableau to meet or exceed the world's defense number. The chooser bonus is to draw one card after settling.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Military Strength
walkthrough.text: Military strength is the sum of all military icons across the cards in your tableau. If your tableau shows a total military of four, you can settle any military world with a defense of four or less for free — no cards discarded. Some cards provide temporary military bonuses that apply for a single settle action. Military worlds are powerful because they cost zero cards to play once you have the strength.

walkthrough.title: Phase Four: Consume
walkthrough.text: During Consume, you activate consume powers on cards in your tableau. Each consume power specifies what type of good it can consume and what you get in return — usually VP chips. Consuming is mandatory: if you have goods and consume powers available, you must use them. There are two Consume action cards to choose from.

walkthrough.title: Consume Variants
walkthrough.text: Consume Trade lets you sell one good for cards before consuming. You draw cards equal to the good's trade value. Then you consume any remaining goods as normal. Consume two-times VP doubles all VP chips you earn during this phase. Both variants are powerful in different situations. Trade is great for drawing cards, while two-times VP accelerates your scoring.

walkthrough.title: Phase Five: Produce
walkthrough.text: During Produce, you place one good on each of your production worlds that does not already have a good. A good is represented by a face-down card from the draw deck placed on the world. Windfall worlds do not produce during the normal Produce phase — they only get a good when first settled. The chooser bonus lets you also produce on one windfall world.

walkthrough.title: Production and Windfall Worlds
walkthrough.text: Production worlds have a solid colored halo and produce a good every Produce phase automatically. Windfall worlds have an outlined colored halo and receive one good when settled, but do not produce again unless you have a special ability. The color of the halo tells you what type of good the world produces — blue for novelty, brown for rare, green for genes, and yellow for alien.

walkthrough.title: Good Types and Colors
walkthrough.text: There are four types of goods, distinguished by the halo color on production and windfall worlds. Blue goods are novelty goods. Brown goods are rare elements. Green goods are genes. Yellow goods are alien technology. The type matters because consume powers often specify which good types they can consume, and trade values differ by type. Alien goods typically have the highest trade values.

walkthrough.title: Six-Cost Developments
walkthrough.text: The most expensive cards in the game are six-cost developments. These are major investments — you discard six cards to play one, or five if you chose the Develop phase. But they are worth it because they provide huge endgame VP bonuses based on your tableau composition. One might score bonus points for every military world you own, another for production worlds. Plan your entire game around these if you draw one early.

walkthrough.title: End of Game Triggers
walkthrough.text: The game can end in two ways. First, if any player places their twelfth card in their tableau, the game ends after the current round is complete. Second, if the VP chip pool runs out during a Consume phase, the game ends after the current round. When either trigger occurs, finish all active phases in the current round, then proceed to scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Add up victory points from three sources. First, sum the VP printed on every card in your tableau — both developments and worlds have VP values. Second, add all VP chips you accumulated from Consume phases during the game. Third, calculate any endgame bonus VP from six-cost developments based on your tableau composition. The player with the highest total wins. If tied, the player with the most cards in hand plus goods on worlds breaks the tie.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Galaxy
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three example players. Each player has a different starting world and will take a different strategic approach. The simultaneous play means everyone acts at once, so follow along with all three players. Let's explore the galaxy!

walkthrough.title: Starting Tableaux
walkthrough.text: Player one has a starting world that provides one military strength. Player two has a production world that produces blue novelty goods. Player three has a development-focused starting world with a card draw bonus. Each player also has four cards in hand after discarding two during setup.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Phase Selection
walkthrough.text: All three players look at their hands and secretly choose an action card. Player one picks Explore plus one plus one to draw more cards. Player two picks Produce to get a good on their production world. Player three picks Develop to play a cheap development. All three reveal simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Phase One: Explore Resolves
walkthrough.text: Player one draws three cards and keeps two — that is their chooser bonus. Players two and three also get to explore because the phase is active, but they only get the base action: draw two, keep one. Everyone has more cards in hand now.

walkthrough.title: Why Everyone Benefits
walkthrough.text: Notice what happened — Player one chose Explore, but all three players got cards from it. This is the core tension in Race for the Galaxy. Every phase you choose helps your opponents too. The skill is choosing phases that benefit you more than they benefit everyone else.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two: Develop Resolves
walkthrough.text: Player three plays a one-cost development from their hand, discarding zero cards because of the chooser bonus (one-card discount on a one-cost card). Players one and two could also develop, but Player one does not have a development they want to play, and Player two decides to save their cards.

walkthrough.title: How Payment Works
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and make sure you understand payment. When Player three played that development, they would normally discard one card from their hand as payment. But because they were the chooser, they got a one-card discount, making it free. If Player two had wanted to play that same development, they would have had to discard one card. Cards you discard as payment are gone — choose carefully what you spend.

walkthrough.title: Phase Five: Produce Resolves
walkthrough.text: Player two has a blue production world, so they take the top card of the draw deck and place it face down on that world. That face-down card represents a blue novelty good. Players one and three would also produce if they had production worlds, but neither does yet.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Goods
walkthrough.text: That face-down card on Player two's world is a good. It sits there until it is consumed or traded during a future Consume phase. You cannot look at goods — they are just markers. Goods are your main source of VP chips and card draws, so producing and consuming efficiently is how you score points.

walkthrough.title: Player One's Mistake: Wrong Phase
walkthrough.text: Player one chose Explore, which gave everyone cards. But Player one's starting world has military strength, and they have a military world in hand they could settle for free. If Player one had chosen Settle instead, they would have placed a military world (for free!) and drawn a bonus card as the chooser. Instead, they gave everyone extra cards while missing an opportunity to expand their tableau.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Read Your Starting World
walkthrough.text: Your starting world tells you what strategy to pursue. A military starting world means you should look for military worlds to settle for free. A production starting world means you should set up a Produce-Consume engine. A development-focused world means you should develop early. Do not ignore what your starting world is telling you — it is your biggest clue for the first few rounds.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Phase Selection
walkthrough.text: A new round begins. All players take back their action cards. Player one now chooses Settle to play that military world. Player two chooses Consume Trade to sell their good for cards. Player three chooses Explore plus five to dig for more options. Let's see what happens.

walkthrough.title: Phase One: Explore Resolves Again
walkthrough.text: Player three chose Explore plus five, so Explore is active. Player three draws seven cards and keeps one — lots of options but they can only keep the best. Players one and two draw two and keep one each. Everyone benefits from Player three's choice.

walkthrough.title: Phase Three: Settle Resolves
walkthrough.text: Player one chose Settle. They reveal a military world with a defense of two from their hand. Player one's tableau has one military strength from their starting world. That is not enough! They need at least two military to settle this world. Player one cannot play it and wastes their phase selection.

walkthrough.title: Player One's Mistake: Insufficient Military
walkthrough.text: Player one tried to settle a world they could not afford. Their military strength was one, but the world required two. Unlike non-military worlds where you can overpay with extra cards, military worlds have a strict threshold — you either meet the defense number or you do not. Always count your military icons before choosing Settle for a military world.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Count Before You Commit
walkthrough.text: Before selecting a phase, make sure you can actually do what you plan. For military worlds, count every military icon in your tableau and check if it meets the defense requirement. For developments and non-military worlds, count the cards in your hand and check if you have enough to pay after accounting for any chooser discount. Planning a move you cannot execute wastes your phase selection.

walkthrough.title: Phase Four: Consume Trade Resolves
walkthrough.text: Player two chose Consume Trade. They sell their blue novelty good first, drawing cards equal to its trade value. Then they check for any remaining consume powers — they have none yet, so the phase ends. Player two traded one good for several cards, refilling their hand nicely.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Mistake: No Consume Powers
walkthrough.text: Player two traded successfully, which is great. But notice that after trading, they had no consume powers to convert remaining goods to VP chips. If they had settled a world or developed a card with consume powers before this round, they could have scored VP chips in addition to drawing cards. Building consume powers before you start consuming is essential.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Your Consume Engine First
walkthrough.text: Producing goods is pointless unless you can consume them. Before you start a Produce-Consume cycle, make sure your tableau has cards with consume powers that match your good types. The most common mistake is producing goods that sit unused because you have no way to convert them into points.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You saw how phase selection works, how cards serve as currency, and how military and production strategies differ. You watched Player one learn about matching phases to their starting world and counting military strength, and Player two learn about building consume powers before producing.

walkthrough.title: Before You Launch
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics like reading your starting world and building engines.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to phases, card types, and iconography.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Launch
bullets: Check out General Tips → engine-building and phase reading
Reference the Appendix → phases, card types, and iconography
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Read Your Starting World
walkthrough.text: Your starting world is the single biggest clue about which strategy to pursue. A military starting world means you should look for military worlds to settle for free. A production world means you should build toward a Produce-Consume loop. A development-focused world means you should invest in cheap developments early. Do not fight your starting world — lean into what it offers.

walkthrough.title: Plan a Consume Engine
walkthrough.text: The most reliable scoring path in Race for the Galaxy is producing goods and consuming them for VP chips. To make this work, you need three things: production worlds to generate goods, consume powers on your cards to convert goods into VP chips, and a rhythm of alternating Produce and Consume phases. Build all three elements before you start cycling.

walkthrough.title: Keep Flexible Cards Early
walkthrough.text: In the first two or three rounds, keep cards that work in multiple strategies rather than committing too hard to one path. You might draw a game-changing card that shifts your direction. The exception is your starting world — you should always lean toward its natural strategy. But within that framework, stay flexible until you see which six-cost developments or key worlds appear.

walkthrough.title: Watch Opponents' Phase Choices
walkthrough.text: Predict which phases your opponents will choose so you can get free actions. If Player two has production worlds full of goods, they are probably going to choose Consume soon. That means you can plan to consume as well without wasting your own phase selection on it. If everyone at the table needs to Explore, let someone else choose it while you pick a phase that only benefits you.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Overspend Key Cards
walkthrough.text: The most painful mistake in Race for the Galaxy is discarding a card as payment that you needed to play later. Before paying for anything, check your entire hand and ask yourself: is any card here more valuable played than spent? Sometimes it is better to wait a round and explore for payment cards rather than gutting your hand to play something now.

walkthrough.title: Six-Cost Developments Are Game-Changers
walkthrough.text: Six-cost developments provide massive endgame VP bonuses based on your tableau composition. If you draw one early, build your entire strategy around qualifying for its bonus. They are expensive — six cards to play, or five with the chooser discount — but a well-matched six-cost development can score eight to twelve bonus points. That is often the difference between winning and losing.

walkthrough.title: Pace Yourself on Military
walkthrough.text: Military strength lets you settle military worlds for free, which is extremely efficient. But military icons themselves do not score VP. You need to balance military investment with actual scoring mechanisms. Build enough military to settle the worlds you want, then pivot to producing and consuming. Do not keep stacking military strength past what you need.

walkthrough.title: Phase Selection Efficiency
walkthrough.text: The best phase selections benefit you significantly more than your opponents. Choosing Settle when you are the only one with a world ready to play is ideal. Choosing Explore when everyone needs cards is inefficient — you get a small bonus while giving everyone else free cards. Always ask: how much does this help me versus how much does it help the table?

summary.title: Read Your Starting World
bullets: Military start → settle military worlds for free
Production start → build Produce/Consume engine
Development start → invest in cheap devs early

summary.title: Plan a Consume Engine
bullets: Need: production worlds + consume powers + Produce/Consume rhythm
Most reliable VP scoring path in the game
Build all 3 elements before cycling

bullets: Don't over-commit in rounds 1-3

summary.title: Watch Opponents' Phase Choices

Let others choose Explore when everyone needs cards
Pick phases that benefit you uniquely

summary.title: Do Not Overspend Key Cards

summary.title: Six-Cost Developments Are Game-Changers

summary.title: Pace Yourself on Military

summary.title: Phase Selection Efficiency
bullets: Best picks: benefit you much more than opponents
Worst picks: help the whole table equally
Always ask: how much does this help me vs. the table?

walkthrough.title: The Produce-Consume Engine
walkthrough.text: The Produce-Consume loop is the most consistent scoring strategy. Build two or three production worlds and cards with consume powers that match their good types. Each round, alternate between choosing Produce and Consume two-times VP. With three goods consumed at doubled VP, you can earn six or more VP chips per cycle. The key is getting your engine running before the VP pool depletes.

walkthrough.title: The Military Rush
walkthrough.text: Strategy aims to place twelve cards as fast as possible to trigger the game end. Military worlds cost zero cards, so each one you settle is pure tableau expansion. Stack military strength quickly, then settle one world per round for free. You win by ending the game before opponents' engines mature. Pair this with a six-cost development that scores bonus VP for military worlds.

walkthrough.title: The Development Rush
walkthrough.text: Similar to the military rush but focused on developments. Choose Develop frequently to get the one-card discount. Play cheap developments to fill your tableau quickly. Aim for a six-cost development that rewards having many developments. This approach works well when your starting world has development-friendly abilities. Speed is key — trigger the twelve-card ending before opponents can build their engines.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Phase Economy
walkthrough.text: Advanced players treat phase selection as a metagame. In a two-player game, only two phases activate per round, making each selection critical. In a four-player game, three or four phases might activate, giving more free actions. Learn to predict opponents' choices based on their tableaux. If an opponent has goods sitting on worlds, they will likely choose Consume soon. Time your actions to ride their phase selections for free.

walkthrough.title: Card Flow Management
walkthrough.text: Managing your hand size is critical. You need cards to pay for plays, but you also need cards to play. The sweet spot is maintaining four to six cards in hand at all times. If you drop below three, you cannot afford to play anything meaningful. If you hoard too many, you are sitting on potential plays that could be scoring points. Use Explore and Consume Trade to refill, and time your expensive plays when your hand is largest.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Timing
walkthrough.text: Knowing when to trigger the endgame is a crucial skill. If your engine is ahead, race to twelve tableau cards or deplete the VP pool to end the game before opponents catch up. If you are behind, stall — avoid choosing phases that accelerate the end. Do not choose Settle if it would give an opponent their twelfth card. Do not choose Consume if the VP pool is almost empty and your opponent has more goods. Control the pace.

walkthrough.title: Six-Cost Development Optimization
walkthrough.text: The six-cost developments are the highest-leverage cards in the game. If you draw one, immediately assess how many qualifying cards you already have and could realistically add. A six-cost dev that scores two VP per military world is worth fourteen points if you have seven military worlds. Plan your entire mid and late game around maximizing its bonus. If you cannot get at least six to eight bonus VP from a six-cost dev, it may not be worth the investment.

walkthrough.title: Windfall World Exploitation
walkthrough.text: Windfall worlds give you one free good when settled but do not produce again normally. Advanced players exploit them by choosing Produce as the chooser — the bonus lets you produce on one windfall world per Produce phase. Stack two or three windfall worlds, settle them for their instant goods, then use the chooser bonus to keep one producing each round. Combine with Consume two-times VP for big scoring turns.

summary.title: The Produce-Consume Engine
bullets: Build 2-3 production worlds + matching consume powers
Alternate Produce and Consume: 2x VP each round
3 goods × 2x VP = 6+ VP chips per cycle

summary.title: The Military Rush
bullets: Stack military → settle worlds for free
Race to 12 tableau cards to end the game early
Pair with 6-cost dev scoring military worlds

summary.title: The Development Rush
bullets: Choose Develop frequently for 1-card discount
Fill tableau quickly with cheap developments
End game before opponents' engines mature

summary.title: Reading the Phase Economy
bullets: Predict opponents' phases → ride their selections for free
2P = only 2 phases/round (critical), 4P = 3-4 phases (more free actions)
Opponents with goods will likely choose Consume

summary.title: Card Flow Management
bullets: Maintain 4-6 cards in hand at all times
Below 3 = can't afford meaningful plays
Use Explore and Trade to refill before big plays

summary.title: Endgame Timing
bullets: Ahead: race to 12 cards or deplete VP pool
Behind: stall, avoid phases that accelerate end
Don't give opponents their 12th card via Settle

summary.title: Six-Cost Development Optimization
bullets: Highest-leverage cards in the game
Need 6-8+ bonus VP to justify the investment
Plan mid/late game entirely around maximizing bonus

summary.title: Windfall World Exploitation
bullets: Windfall worlds give 1 free good on settle
Chooser of Produce can produce on 1 windfall per round
Stack windfall worlds + Consume: 2x VP = big scoring

entries.definition: The card-drawing phase. Base action: draw two cards, keep one. Chooser variant Explore +5 draws seven and keeps one. Chooser variant Explore +1,+1 draws three and keeps two.
Play one development card by discarding cards from hand equal to its cost. Chooser gets a one-card discount. Development costs range from one to six.
Play one world card. Non-military worlds cost cards from hand. Military worlds require military strength meeting or exceeding defense. Chooser draws one card after settling.
Activate consume powers to convert goods into VP chips or cards. Mandatory when possible. Two chooser variants: Consume Trade (sell one good for cards first) and Consume 2x VP (double all VP chips).
Place one good (face-down card) on each production world without a good. Windfall worlds do not produce. Chooser can also produce on one windfall world.
A card type with a diamond-shaped cost icon. Played during the Develop phase by discarding cards as payment. Developments provide ongoing abilities and VP.
A world card with a circle cost icon. Settled during the Settle phase by discarding cards from hand equal to its cost number.
A world card with a red circle and defense number. Settled for free if your total military strength meets or exceeds the defense value. No cards discarded.
A world with a solid colored halo. Automatically produces one good during every Produce phase if it does not already have a good on it.
A world with an outlined colored halo. Receives one good when first settled but does not produce during normal Produce phases. The chooser of Produce can produce on one windfall world.

The most expensive development type. Costs six cards to play (five with chooser discount). Provides significant endgame VP bonuses based on tableau composition, such as bonus VP per military world or per production world.
One of five special world cards dealt randomly during setup. Forms the first card in your tableau and shapes your opening strategy. Each provides different starting abilities.
A good produced by blue-haloed worlds. Novelty goods are the most common type. Trade value varies by card.
A good produced by brown-haloed worlds. Rare elements have moderate trade values.
A good produced by green-haloed worlds. Genes goods have higher trade values than novelty or rare elements.
A good produced by yellow-haloed worlds. Alien goods typically have the highest trade values in the game.
The cumulative total of all military icons across cards in your tableau. Used to settle military worlds without discarding cards. Some cards provide temporary military for a single settle action.
The enhanced benefit received by the player who selected a particular phase. Examples: Develop gets one-card discount, Settle draws one card after, Produce can produce on one windfall.
The core economic mechanic. Cards serve dual purpose — they can be played into your tableau or discarded as payment for other cards. There is no separate currency in the game.
All players act simultaneously within each phase. Phase selection is secret, creating a guessing and reading metagame about what opponents will choose.
An ability on a tableau card that converts a good into VP chips or other benefits. Consume powers specify which good types they accept and must be used if available.
The Consume Trade variant lets you sell one good before consuming. You draw cards equal to the good's trade value, printed on the world card.
Your personal area of played cards, including your starting world, settled worlds, and developed cards. Tableau size triggers endgame at twelve cards.
The shared supply of victory point chips (twelve per player). When this pool runs out during Consume, the game ends after the current round.
Each player's set of seven phase selection cards. One is chosen secretly each round. The two Explore variants and two Consume variants provide different chooser bonuses.
The game ends when either a player places their twelfth tableau card or the VP chip pool runs out. Finish the current round, then score.
Total VP equals tableau card VP plus accumulated VP chips plus six-cost development endgame bonuses. Highest total wins. Tiebreaker: most cards in hand plus goods on worlds.
Victory points printed on each development and world in your tableau. One of the three final scoring categories.
Points earned during Consume phases throughout the game. Accumulated from chip pool. One of the three final scoring categories.
Additional VP from six-cost developments based on tableau composition. Can provide eight to twelve or more bonus points. One of the three final scoring categories.
